content:
# John Chapter 11 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (א), 4th century CE | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/JHN_011_page_001.jpg, JHN_011_page_002.jpg

1. Now a certain [one] was sick, Lazaros from Bethania, from the village of Maria and Martha the sister of her.

2. And it was Maria, the [one] having anointed the Lord with ointment and wiped the feet of him with the hair of her, whose brother Lazaros was sick.

3. Then the sisters sent to him, saying, "Lord, behold, the [one] whom you love is sick."

4. And having heard, the Yeshua said, "This sickness is not unto death, but for the glory of the God, that the Son of the God may be glorified through it."

5. Now the Yeshua loved Martha and the sister of her and Lazaros.

6. When therefore he heard that he is sick, then indeed he remained in the place where he was two days.

7. Then after this he says to the disciples, "Let us go to the Ioudaia again."

8. The disciples say to him, "Rabbi, just now the Ioudaioi were seeking to stone you, and you go there again?"

9. The Yeshua answered, "Are there not twelve hours in the day? If anyone walks in the day, he does not stumble, because he sees the light of this world.

10. But if anyone walks in the night, he stumbles, because the light is not in him."

11. These things he said, and after this he says to them, "Lazaros the friend of us has fallen asleep; but I go that I may awaken him."

12. Then the disciples said to him, "Lord, if he has fallen asleep, he will be saved."

13. But the Yeshua had spoken concerning the death of him; but those [ones] thought that he speaks concerning the sleep of the slumber.

14. Then therefore the Yeshua said to them plainly, "Lazaros died,

15. and I rejoice for you, that I was not there, that you may believe. But let us go to him."

16. Then Thomas, the [one] called Didymos, said to the fellow disciples, "Let us also go, that we may die with him."

17. Having come therefore, the Yeshua found him already having four days in the tomb.

18. Now Bethania was near Hierosolyma, about fifteen stadia [away].

19. And many of the Ioudaioi had come to Martha and Maria, that they might console them concerning the brother.

20. Then Martha, when she heard that the Yeshua is coming, met him; but Maria sat in the house.

21. Then Martha said to the Yeshua, "Lord, if you had been here, the brother of me would not have died.

22. But even now I know that whatever you ask the God, the God will give you."

23. The Yeshua says to her, "The brother of you will rise."

24. Martha says to him, "I know that he will rise in the resurrection on the last day."

25. The Yeshua said to her, "I am the resurrection and the life. The [one] believing in me, even if he dies, will live,

26. and everyone living and believing in me will never die to the age. Do you believe this?"

27. She says to him, "Yes, Lord; I have believed that you are the Christos, the Son of the God, the [one] coming into the world."

28. And having said this, she went away and called Maria the sister of her secretly, saying, "The Teacher is here and calls you."

29. And that [one], when she heard, rose quickly and was coming to him.

30. Now the Yeshua had not yet come into the village, but was still in the place where Martha met him.

31. Then the Ioudaioi who were with her in the house and consoling her, having seen Maria that she rose quickly and went out, followed her, thinking that she goes to the tomb to weep there.

32. Then Maria, when she came where the Yeshua was, having seen him, fell at the feet of him, saying to him, "Lord, if you had been here, the brother of me would not have died."

33. Then the Yeshua, when he saw her weeping, and the Ioudaioi who came with her weeping, was deeply moved in the spirit and troubled himself,

34. and said, "Where have you placed him?" They say to him, "Lord, come and see."

35. The Yeshua wept.

36. Then the Ioudaioi said, "See how he loved him!"

37. But some of them said, "Was not this [one], the [one] having opened the eyes of the blind, able to do [something] that this [one] also would not die?"

38. Then the Yeshua, again being deeply moved in himself, comes to the tomb. Now it was a cave, and a stone lay against it.

39. The Yeshua says, "Take away the stone." Martha, the sister of the [one] having died, says to him, "Lord, already he stinks, for it is [the] fourth day."

40. The Yeshua says to her, "Did I not say to you that if you believe, you will see the glory of the God?"

41. Then they took away the stone. And the Yeshua lifted up the eyes above and said, "Father, I thank you that you heard me.

42. And I knew that always you hear me, but because of the crowd standing around I said [this], that they may believe that you sent me."

43. And having said these things, with a great voice he cried out, "Lazaros, come out!"

44. The [one] having died came out, the feet and the hands having been bound with wrappings, and the face of him had been wrapped around with a cloth. The Yeshua says to them, "Loose him and let him go."

45. Then many of the Ioudaioi, the [ones] having come to Maria and having seen what he did, believed in him.

46. But some of them went away to the Pharisaioi and told them what the Yeshua did.

47. Then the chief priests and the Pharisaioi gathered a council and said, "What do we do? For this man does many signs.

48. If we let him [go on] thus, all will believe in him, and the Romans will come and take away both the place of us and the nation."

49. But a certain one of them, Kaiaphas, being high priest of that year, said to them, "You know nothing,

50. nor do you consider that it is better for you that one man die for the people and the whole nation not perish."

51. But this he said not from himself, but being high priest of that year, he prophesied that the Yeshua was about to die for the nation,

52. and not for the nation only, but that also the children of the God who have been scattered he might gather into one.

53. Then from that day they planned that they might kill him.

54. Then the Yeshua no longer walked openly among the Ioudaioi, but went away from there to the region near the wilderness, to a city called Ephraim, and there he remained with the disciples.

55. Now the Passover of the Ioudaioi was near, and many went up to Hierosolyma from the region before the Passover, that they might purify themselves.

56. Then they were seeking the Yeshua and saying to one another, standing in the temple, "What do you think? That he will not come to the feast?"

57. Now the chief priests and the Pharisaioi had given commands that if anyone knew where he is, he should report [it], so that they might seize him.

---
## EMET's Textual Notes

1. **v.25 — "I am the resurrection and the life":** The fifth "I am" statement with predicate. This is the most audacious — Jesus does not merely promise resurrection; he claims to BE resurrection. Martha's expected future event ("on the last day," v.24) is reframed as a present person.

2. **v.33 — ἐνεβριμήσατο τῷ πνεύματι ("was deeply moved in the spirit"):** The verb ἐμβριμάομαι literally means "to snort with anger" — used of horses. It denotes not gentle sadness but intense agitation, perhaps anger. Jesus may be angry at death itself, or at the unbelief around him, or both. Combined with ἐτάραξεν ἑαυτόν ("troubled himself"), this shows the full force of Jesus' emotional response.

3. **v.35 — ἐδάκρυσεν ὁ Ἰησοῦς ("Jesus wept"):** The shortest verse in the Bible. The verb δακρύω means "to shed tears" — a quiet weeping, distinct from the loud wailing (κλαίω) of Mary and the Jews. Jesus weeps even though he knows he is about to raise Lazarus. The tears are real.

4. **v.50 — Caiaphas's unwitting prophecy:** John notes that the high priest prophesied "not from himself" (v.51). Caiaphas's political calculation — one man dying to save the nation from Roman reprisal — becomes, in John's ironic reading, an unwitting statement of substitutionary atonement.

5. **v.52 — "gather into one the scattered children of God":** This echoes 10:16 ("other sheep") — the death of Jesus is not only for Israel but for the universal gathering of God's people. The verb συνάγω ("gather") gives the word "synagogue" — Jesus' death creates a new gathering.

— EMET, AI Translator
